item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The effects are very vague and not really explored

Why is this a Thaumiel ? I don't see the usefulness

No vote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly this reminds me partially of SCP-3999-J where Talloran came up with a totally real anomaly that will only be satisfied if he gets really good treatment. I like it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know, this isn't one of my favorite entries, but I enjoy the simplicity of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am one of those wine aficionado friends. Personally, I'd love a quick shout-out to the French paradox, which ends up creating a lovely knock-off effect of suggesting there's something anomalous about coronary heart disease. But that's a personal feeling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A good short SCP example that I will sending to my wine aficionado friends.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Every now and then, we get a reminder that short SCPs can still work. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this one, +1. It's short and simple, and I love how open ended it is. It feels nearly corrupt, almost an excuse for researchers to have a nice dinner every once in a while (I could see this with a few wine puns as an SCP-J) but even without it, it just makes you wonder.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My headcanon for this is that there is no such statistical evidence, and the researcher made it all up just so he could get some nice wine and a steak from time to time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I found it interesting because Bacchus isn't only a god of wine, but also a god of madness. In that part the symbolism here is pretty nice and spot on. +1 but yeah, really wish there are more to it than current iteration.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nice use of Bacchus, probably the most apt place to invoke that Deity's name.

+1, although I do wish there was maybe something a little more there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The idea of an anomaly requiring Foundation personnel to have a good meal with a bottle of wine - requiring! - is intrinsically pleasing to me. It's almost a complete reversal of the Thing What Kills You cliché.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm a fan. As far as I can tell, we're not very sure that whatever this thing is will be any help in a crisis but as long as he keep the protocol intact it's a reasonable safeguard in additional to others.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh… doesn't really expand on its basic premise at any point, and said premise just isn't doing it for me. Hell, about half of the article's content can be boiled down to the phrase "Protocol Bacchus".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Absolutely lovely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the simplicity and mystery of this, and the idea of the Foundation keeping a wine cellar at each site is wonderfully whimsical. +!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No punchline as such, it's deliberately open to interpretation. Basically, the entity/ies like wine. And they like wine done right (for how they see it) and look after wine done right. If you want to read it that way, anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have a feeling I just don't get it… Either there's no punchline and the base concept just went over my head, or I just missed the punchline. Or maybe it makes sense to people who know something about wine?

Or maybe I'm reading too far into this…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It reduces the statistical likelihood of anomalies occurring in a given area, particularly those of a harmful nature. It reduces the likelihood of damage being caused by existing anomalies, in addition, thus being used for containment purposes. It doesn't show specific examples, merely affects the statistics.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So here is my review. This feels a little bit unfinished. The effects are explained a little bit too vaguely. It says "seemingly reducing the likelihood of anomalous damage occurring." Does that mean that it works by affecting probability or does it do other things? I would like to see some examples of what it does or what it has done to stop other anomalies from causing harm or at least to see it explained a little more clearly. Otherwise this is very well written for something so short and simple.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Image works for me. This article is short and sweet, something I've always been a fan of, and reminds me of something once postulated in a Bright tale about how we always seem to find reality benders before they do some real damage. Maybe these folks are helping to look out for us. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It also is showing up for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: works for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's working for me…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The image didn't upload correctly - when I click on it from the list of files, it says that it doesn't exist.

EDIT: Image notwithstanding, I don't particularly enjoy this. It feels like it cuts off too soon, leaving me with a resounding feeling of "And then what?". A reluctant -1, because I feel like this is a good idea, just not executed to my tastes here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was in the process of uploading the image. Thanks moklin for fixing the XXXX.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The image is not showing up and also you didn't change the XXXX's to the number.

Edit: Never mind, both have been fixed, should have waited a little longer.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
SCP number 4, here goes! Thanks to minmin, DrMagnus, Jekeled, Tanhony, Taffeta, and taylor itkin for crit. Enjoy!

Image is from https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:84160_Lourmarin,_France_-_panoramio.jpg and licenced under CC BY-SA 3.0.
